results.possibleIdentity: Jane McCarthy was a computer programmer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s who was a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded by engineers Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer and artists Robert Rauschenberg and Robert Whitman to foster collaborations between the arts and technology. Her professional background was as a programmer for Pan American World Airways (Pan Am) in New York City, as recorded in E.A.T.'s technical services registry. This registry, which used a system of index cards to categorize members, listed her technical expertise in programming and her employer as Pan American. During her period of activity, Pan Am's computing operations were centered in the Pan Am Building in Manhattan, utilizing the PANAMAC reservations system. She was one of the technical professionals who volunteered their skills to assist artists with technology-based projects through E.A.T.'s matching service. The organization was notable for major projects like '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' (1966) and the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70, as well as 'Telex: Q&A' (1971), which utilized the global telex networks of companies like Pan Am to facilitate cross-cultural communication.
